reason: The paper explicitly computes "Feature importance scores for each residue position... based on the total decrease in node gini-impurity averaged over the 500 trees" (ranger package), ranks residues by these scores, and uses that ranking (via incremental feature selection) to select the "top 22 residue positions." The central biological conclusion, "the majority of highest scoring residues (17 out of 22 residues) reside within the N-terminal hot-dog structure," and the claim that 5-16 new candidate residues (e.g., I52, L59, A63, L64, V67) govern substrate specificity, depend directly on which specific residues the gini-importance ranking placed at the top. The only statistical treatment applied is the ranger "importance_pvalues" (Altmann) permutation-of-response test with FDR correction, which addresses whether an importance score is significantly non-zero, not whether the relative magnitude/ranking among correlated or interacting residue positions is unbiased. The subsequent 10-fold cross-validation only reports overall recall/specificity/MCC of nested cumulative-feature models to find a performance plateau, which is exactly the kind of "overall model accuracy on a held-out set" that the rubric says does not validate a ranking (no random-subset baseline or alternative importance/conditional-importance method was used to check whether a different set of 22 residues would perform as well). Only 6 of the 22 selected residues have independent prior experimental (site-directed mutagenesis) confirmation; the other 16, including all novel claims, rest solely on the uncorroborated gini-importance ranking, and the discussion's appeal to structural plausibility (proximity to the hot-dog domain/active site) is a post-hoc narrative fit rather than a formal corroboration method (permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance).
